prior.content_md:
## Modal Shift as a Cost and Emissions Strategy

For cargo verticals where scheduling permits, air-to-ocean modal shift eliminates SAF surcharge exposure entirely for the shifted portion of freight. Ocean routes from EU ports are subject to FuelEU Maritime (Regulation (EU) 2023/1805), which imposes separate GHG intensity reduction requirements on maritime operators; those costs are distinct from and currently lower per-unit than air SAF costs.

The workspace's assessment of modal shift viability should be conducted vertical by vertical:
- **Live events, film and TV, humanitarian:** Typically non-negotiable for air due to operational deadlines. Modal shift not viable for most shipments.
- **Luxury goods, high-value automotive parts:** Scheduling flexibility exists for some shipments. Air-to-ocean analysis warranted where delivery windows exceed ocean transit times on applicable trade lanes.
- **Fine art:** Mixed. Insured transit value, climate-controlled requirements, and loan deadline constraints typically favour air, but select consolidation opportunities on less time-critical collection movements may tolerate ocean.

Cost figures for the air-to-ocean modal shift comparison on specific trade lanes are not publicly available in sources accessed for this brief. The workspace should conduct lane-by-lane analysis using current carrier rate sheets.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1805 (FuelEU Maritime), Official Journal of the European Union, 22 September 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1805/oj/eng (context on ocean cost exposure as an alternative)*

## SAF Voluntary Procurement (Book-and-Claim)

Some airlines offer voluntary SAF book-and-claim programmes enabling cargo customers to purchase SAF certificates to cover their shipment's attributed emissions, independent of the airline's regulatory mandate compliance. This creates a commercial opportunity for the workspace to offer clients a "SAF-covered shipment" product on any trade lane, not only EU-origin flights subject to the mandate.

The integrity of book-and-claim certificates depends on the certification body and methodology used. No standardised EU-level certification framework for voluntary SAF book-and-claim for cargo customers has been published in the sources available for this brief. The workspace should verify certification methodology before making client-facing claims. Industry operator interpretation: IATA has published guidance on SAF book-and-claim frameworks; this is cited for navigation only and not as legal authority.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/2405, Recitals and Article 3(5) (SAF eligibility criteria establishing the floor for regulatory SAF quality), Official Journal of the European Union, 31 October 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/2405/oj/eng*

---
